title: Dynamic Many-Body Theory: The dynamics of atomic impurities in $^4$He
abstract: We implement manifestly microscopic many-body methods to study the dynamics of atomic impurities in a host quantum fluid, specifically $^4$He. Our investigations are motivated by experiments of muonium atoms within $^4$He with the goal of testing the universality of free fall by neutral bound states using unstable particles.   Structure calculations are performed using standard semi-analytic methods; we extend here the calculations of our previos work (Journal of Low Temperature Physics {\bf 93}, 415-449 (1993)) to muonic \he4, antiprotonic \he4 and mounium atoms within \he4. We find that the muonium impurity probes, due to its large zero-point motion, the atomic interaction at rather short distances. Its chemical potential is estimated to be about 19 meV. Antiprotonic \he4 has, on the other hand, a negative chemical potential.   Dynamics is treated by making all correlation functions time-dependent. In analogy to the derivation of the dynamics of the background liquid, we derive a working formula for the impurity self-energy that includes the most relevant physical effects. Results for the effective mass of \he3 and hydrogen atoms agree well with available experiments. The dispersion relations of muonic \he4 and antiprotonic \he4 pass through under the roton minimum.
summary: This paper develops a microscopic theory to study how different atomic impurities move through superfluid helium-4. It specifically highlights how the large zero-point motion of light particles like muonium leads to massive increases in effective mass and unique dispersion relations.
main_problem: The study aims to develop a Dynamic Many-Body Theory to describe the dynamics, energetics, and effective mass of various atomic impurities (such as muonium, antiprotonic helium, and hydrogen isotopes) embedded in a superfluid 4He host.
main_result: The theory predicts extreme mass enhancement for muonium (m*/m > 100) due to large zero-point motion and shows that the dispersion relations for muonic and antiprotonic helium pass under the roton minimum.
method: The authors use a microscopic many-body approach based on the Jastrow-Feenberg ansatz, the Hypernetted chain (HNC)/Euler-Lagrange method, and a time-dependent generalization of the variational principle to derive impurity self-energy.
model_or_system: A single atomic impurity (Mu, p-bar, H, D, T, or 3He) immersed in a bulk 4He quantum liquid, modeled with Aziz-type He-He potentials and impurity-host potentials characterized by a strong repulsive core.
key_observables: Chemical potential, effective mass, dispersion relations, impurity self-energy, pair distribution function, and particle lifetime.
important_parameters: deBoer parameter (quantumness), reduced mass, scattering length, and the phonon-roton spectrum of the host liquid.
main_assumptions: The theory utilizes the G0W approximation for dispersion relations and a three-phonon vertex approximation to preserve long-wavelength properties; the Feynman approximation for the host spectrum is noted to be a factor of 2 too high.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows interaction potentials and scattering wave functions; Figure 2 compares chemical potentials vs density; Figure 7 shows effective mass vs density; Figure 8 compares dispersion relations; Figure 9 shows self-energy and particle lifetime; Figure 10 shows the imaginary part of the self-energy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation regarding muonium experiments, defines the many-body Hamiltonian and variational framework, details the derivation of the Dynamic Many-Body Theory, presents results for static properties (chemical potential), and concludes with dynamic properties (effective mass and damping).
why_interesting: It provides a sophisticated microscopic framework for understanding how a light impurity couples to the collective excitations (phonons/rotons) of a quantum fluid, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and impurity physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
